questions.text: What does the abbreviation EER stand for in database modeling?
questions.explanation: According to the chapter outline, EER stands for either Enhanced ER or Extended ER.

questions.text: Which of the following is an additional concept provided by the EER model that is NOT present in the basic ER model?
questions.explanation: Subclasses/superclasses, specialization/generalization, categories, and inheritance are additional EER concepts.

questions.text: If an entity type `EMPLOYEE` is grouped into `SECRETARY`, `ENGINEER`, and `TECHNICIAN`, what are these subgroupings called?
questions.explanation: An entity type may have additional meaningful subgroupings called subclasses or subtypes.

questions.text: The relationship between a subclass and its superclass is often referred to as a(n) ________ relationship.
questions.explanation: Subclass/superclass relationships are called IS-A relationships (e.g., SECRETARY IS-A EMPLOYEE).

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true regarding subclass membership?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An entity can exist in a subclass without being a member of the superclass.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An entity must be a member of the superclass to be a member of a subclass.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every entity in a superclass must belong to at least one subclass.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A subclass member is a different real-world entity than the superclass member.

questions.explanation: An entity cannot exist in the database merely by being a member of a subclass; it must also be a member of the superclass.

questions.text: When a subclass inherits from a superclass, it inherits:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only the attributes of the superclass.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the relationships of the superclass.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both all attributes and all relationships of the superclass.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the primary key of the superclass.

questions.explanation: Inheritance in EER means a subclass member inherits all attributes and all relationships of the entity as a member of the superclass.

questions.text: What is 'Specialization' in the context of EER modeling?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A top-down process of defining a set of subclasses of a superclass.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The process of combining multiple entity types into one.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Defining the primary key for an entity.

questions.explanation: Specialization is the process of defining a set of subclasses based on distinguishing characteristics (top-down process).

questions.text: Attributes that apply only to a specific subclass (e.g., `TypingSpeed` for a `SECRETARY`) are called:
questions.explanation: Attributes of a subclass that are not inherited from the superclass are called specific or local attributes.

questions.text: ________ is the process of identifying common features among several entity types and grouping them into a single superclass.
questions.explanation: Generalization is the reverse of specialization; it is a bottom-up process where common features are generalized into a superclass.

questions.text: In a predicate-defined subclass, how is membership determined?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Automatically by a condition on an attribute of the superclass.

questions.explanation: A predicate-defined (or condition-defined) subclass uses a condition on the superclass attributes to determine membership.

questions.text: If membership in a subclass is determined manually by a user rather than by a condition, the subclass is called:
questions.explanation: In user-defined subclasses, membership is specified individually for each entity by the database user applying an operation.

questions.text: An attribute-defined specialization occurs when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: All subclasses are based on different attributes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All subclasses in a specialization have membership conditions on the same attribute of the superclass.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A subclass has multiple attributes defined locally.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The superclass has only one attribute.

questions.explanation: If all subclasses in a specialization have a membership condition on the same attribute, it is an attribute-defined specialization.

questions.text: Which constraint specifies that an entity can be a member of at most one subclass in a specialization?
questions.explanation: The disjointness constraint specifies that subclasses must be disjoint; an entity can belong to at most one.

questions.text: In an EER diagram, how is a disjoint specialization represented?
questions.explanation: Disjointness is specified by a 'd' in the EER diagram circle.

questions.text: If an entity can belong to more than one subclass of the same specialization, the specialization is:
questions.explanation: If not disjoint, a specialization is overlapping, meaning the same entity can be a member of more than one subclass.

questions.text: The completeness constraint that specifies every entity in the superclass must be a member of at least one subclass is called:
questions.explanation: Total specialization specifies that every entity in the superclass must be a member of some subclass.

questions.text: How is total specialization represented in an EER diagram?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single line connecting the superclass to the circle.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A double line connecting the superclass to the circle.

questions.explanation: Total specialization is shown by a double line, while partial specialization is shown by a single line.

questions.text: A specialization where an entity can exist in the superclass without belonging to any subclass is a:
questions.explanation: Partial specialization allows an entity not to belong to any of the subclasses.

questions.text: Which type of specialization is most common when the superclass is derived from the subclasses (Generalization)?
questions.explanation: Generalization usually results in a total specialization because the superclass is derived from the specific subclasses.

questions.text: A subclass that has more than one superclass is called a:
questions.explanation: A subclass with more than one superclass is called a shared subclass, which leads to multiple inheritance.

questions.text: What is the difference between a specialization hierarchy and a specialization lattice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hierarchy allows multiple inheritance, a lattice does not.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A hierarchy is top-down, a lattice is bottom-up.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A hierarchy allows only single inheritance; a lattice allows multiple inheritance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no difference; they are synonymous.

questions.explanation: In a hierarchy, every subclass has only one superclass (single inheritance). In a lattice, a subclass can have more than one superclass (multiple inheritance).

questions.text: In a lattice, what does a shared subclass inherit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only attributes of its first defined superclass.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only attributes of its direct superclasses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Attributes of its direct superclasses and all their predecessor superclasses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the primary keys of its superclasses.

questions.explanation: A shared subclass inherits attributes of all its direct superclasses as well as all its predecessor superclasses (multiple inheritance).

questions.text: A subclass that represents a collection of entities from a UNION of different entity types is called a:
questions.explanation: A category or UNION type is a subclass that represents a collection of entities from the union of different superclass entity types.

questions.text: What is a key difference between a shared subclass and a category (union type)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shared subclasses use single inheritance; categories use multiple inheritance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A shared subclass member must exist in ALL its superclasses; a category member must exist in AT LEAST ONE.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A shared subclass is a union; a category is an intersection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no difference; they are different names for the same concept.

questions.explanation: A shared subclass is a subset of the *intersection* of its superclasses (must be in all). A category is a subset of the *union* (must be in at least one).

questions.text: Consider a vehicle registration database where an OWNER can be a PERSON, a BANK, or a COMPANY. This is best modeled as a:
questions.explanation: Since an OWNER can be one of several different entity types (PERSON, BANK, COMPANY), this is a category (Union Type).

questions.text: The formal definition of a category \( T \) with superclasses \( D_1, D_2, \dots, D_n \) is:
questions.explanation: A category \( T \) is defined as a subset of the union of its superclasses: \( T \subseteq (D_1 \cup D_2 \cup \dots \cup D_n) \).

questions.text: A specialization \( Z = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n\) with superclass \( G \) is considered **total** if:
questions.explanation: Specialization is total if the union of all subclasses equals the superclass set.

questions.text:
A specialization is formally **disjoint** if for any \( i 
eq j \):
questions.explanation: Disjoint means the intersection of any two distinct subclasses is an empty set.

questions.text: If an attribute-defined specialization is based on a single-valued attribute \( A \), and each subclass \( S_i \) is defined by \( A = c_i \) where all \( c_i \) are distinct, the specialization is automatically:
questions.explanation: If the defining attribute is single-valued and the constant values are distinct, an entity can satisfy only one condition, making it disjoint.

questions.text: Which notation is an alternative to EER diagrams for displaying specialization and generalization?
questions.explanation: UML class diagrams are a popular alternative notation mentioned in the slides for EER concepts.

questions.text: In EER terminology, the word 'Class' can refer to:

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any entity type, superclass, subclass, or category.

questions.explanation: Slide 16 states that all entity types and their corresponding collections (superclasses, subclasses, etc.) can be called 'classes'.

questions.text: The conceptual synthesis process (bottom-up) starts with many entity types and finds common properties. This is called:
questions.explanation: Generalization is described as a bottom-up conceptual synthesis process.

questions.text: Which abstraction concept refers to the relationship between an entity type and the specific entity instances?
questions.explanation: Classification and Instantiation represent the relationship between the class/type and the instances.

questions.text: In EER diagrams, a single line between a superclass and the specialization circle indicates:
questions.explanation: Partial specialization is shown by a single line, allowing an entity to belong to none of the subclasses.

questions.text: True or False: An engineering manager who is both a MANAGER and an ENGINEER belongs to two subclasses of the superclass EMPLOYEE. This is an example of an overlapping specialization.
questions.explanation: Since the entity belongs to more than one subclass in the same context, it is overlapping.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the four types of specialization/generalization?
questions.explanation: The four types are Disjoint Total, Disjoint Partial, Overlapping Total, and Overlapping Partial.

questions.text: In EER modeling, why might one use a 'top-down' approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To combine existing entity types into a common superclass.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To refine an entity type into more specific subclasses (Specialization).

choices.label: C
choices.text: To remove unnecessary attributes from the database.

questions.explanation: Top-down refinement is the process used in specialization to define subclasses of an existing entity type.

questions.text: What is the specific notation for an overlapping constraint in an EER diagram?
questions.explanation: Overlapping is specified by an 'o' in the EER diagram circle.

questions.text: Can a subclass participate in its own specific relationship types, distinct from its superclass?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, it only uses superclass relationships.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, it can participate in local relationship types.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only if it is a shared subclass.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only if it is a total specialization.

questions.explanation: A subclass can participate in specific relationship types (e.g., `HOURLY_EMPLOYEE` participates in `BELONGS_TO`).

questions.text: In a shared subclass (multiple inheritance), if the superclasses have different keys, what is the key of the shared subclass?

choices.label: C
choices.text: It inherits the key from its superclasses (which must represent the same real-world entity).

questions.explanation: The subclass member represents the same real-world entity; thus, it uses the inherited identifying attribute (key) of the superclasses.

questions.text: Which constraint refers to 'Total vs Partial' and 'Exclusive vs Overlapping'?
questions.explanation: Slide 41 lists 'Coverage' as including Total vs. Partial and Exclusive (disjoint) vs. Overlapping.

questions.text: General conceptual modeling concepts include 'Aggregation and Association'. These primarily refer to:
questions.explanation: Slide 41 notes that Aggregation and Association are forms of relationships.

questions.text: True or False: In EER, it is possible for a superclass to be a subclass of another superclass.
questions.explanation: A subclass may itself have further subclasses, forming a hierarchy or lattice.

questions.text: If a specialization is attribute-defined based on 'JobType', and the values for subclasses are 'Secretary', 'Engineer', and 'Technician', what happens to an employee whose 'JobType' is 'Sales'?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The employee is placed in all three subclasses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The employee belongs to the superclass but none of these subclasses.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The employee is automatically a Secretary.

questions.explanation: In a partial specialization (implied if 'Sales' isn't a subclass), an entity doesn't have to belong to any subclass.

questions.text: The concept of 'Inheritance' in EER is most closely related to which programming paradigm?
questions.explanation: Slide 2 mentions that EER includes object-oriented concepts such as inheritance.
